elements.sent-2411-001: In this work we study the behavior of the forward-backward (FB) algorithm when the proximity operator is replaced by a sub-iterative procedure to approximate a Gaussian denoiser, in a Plug-and-Play (PnP) fashion.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, we consider both analysis and synthesis Gaussian denoisers within a dictionary framework, obtained by unrolling dual-FB iterations or FB iterations, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-003: We analyze the associated minimization problems as well as the asymptotic behavior of the resulting FB-PnP iterations.
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, we show that the synthesis Gaussian denoising problem can be viewed as a proximity operator.
elements.sent-2411-005: For each case, analysis and synthesis, we show that the FB-PnP algorithms solve the same problem whether we use only one or an infinite number of sub-iteration to solve the denoising problem at each iteration.
elements.sent-2411-006: To this aim, we show that each "one sub-iteration" strategy within the FB-PnP can be interpreted as a primal-dual algorithm when a warm-restart strategy is used.
elements.sent-2411-007: We further present similar results when using a Moreau-Yosida smoothing of the global problem, for an arbitrary number of sub-iterations.
elements.sent-2411-008: Finally, we provide numerical simulations to illustrate our theoretical results.
elements.sent-2411-009: In particular we first consider a toy compressive sensing example, as well as an image restoration problem in a deep dictionary framework.
elements.sent-2411-010: Plug-and-Play, forward-backward algorithm, unrolling, deep dictionary learning, inverse imaging problems { MSC.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Linear inverse problems play a pivotal role in various scientific disciplines, including imaging~\cite{ribes2008linear , neurosciences~\cite{GKH12 , and astrophysics~\cite{St16 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In these scenarios, an unknown signal $\overline{x \in \Rset^N$ is observed through a degraded linear system given by \begin{equation}\label{pb:invpb} y = A \overline{x} + \varepsilon w,\end{equation} where $y \in \Rset^M$ represents the degraded observations, $A \colon \Rset^N \to \Rset^M$ models a linear measurement operator, $w \in \Rset^M$ is a realization of an i.i.d.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The inverse problem~\eqref{pb:invpb aims to find an estimate $\widehat{x \in \Rset^N$ of $\overline{x}$ from the degraded measurements $y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: This problem is often challenging due to issues such as under-sampling and noise, rendering it ill-posed and/or ill-conditioned.
elements.sent-2411-015: To address these challenges, practitioners commonly employ prior knowledge to guide the selection of a plausible solution.
elements.sent-2411-016: A widely adopted approach involves a maximum a posteriori (MAP) strategy, defining $\widehat{x}$ as a minimizer of a penalized least squares objective: \begin{equation}\label{pb:min_gen} \text{find } \widehat{x} \in \Argmind{x \in \Rset^N} \frac12 \| Ax - y \|^2 + p(x),\end{equation} where $p \colon \Rset^N \to (-\infty, +\infty]$ is a convex, lower semi-continuous, proper function, representing a penalization term that incorporates prior information on the target solution.
elements.sent-2411-017: The choice of the prior is crucial for both reconstruction performance and computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-018: Notably, functions summarizing signal structures based on sparsity have been extensively studied in the literature~\cite{elad2010sparse, bach2012optimization, foucart2013invitation .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Proximal algorithms~\cite{combettes2011proximal, komodakis2015playing are efficient to solve the resulting minimization problem~\eqref{pb:min_gen .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: They are scalable and versatile, offering convergence guarantees and maintaining their status as the state-of-the-art for solving inverse problems for more than two decades.
elements.sent-2411-021: A celebrated proximal algorithm, extensively used in the literature for solving~\eqref{pb:min_gen , is the forward-backward (FB) algorithm, also known as proximal-gradient or ISTA~\cite{Combettes2005 .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: This scheme alternates at each iteration between a gradient step on the differentiable least squares function and a proximal step on the non-smooth function $p$.
elements.sent-2411-023: This algorithm reads \begin{equation}\label{algo:FB} \begin{array}{l} x_0 \in \mathbb R^N \text{for } k = 0, 1, \ldots \left\lfloor \begin{array}{l} x_{k+1} = \prox_{\tau p} \big( x_k - \tau A^*(A x_k - y) \big), \end{array} \right. \end{array}\end{equation} where $\prox_{\tau p}$ is the so-called proximity operator of $p$, and $\tau>0$ is a step-size chosen to ensure the convergence of the generated sequence ${(x_k) _{k\in \Nset}$ to a solution to~\eqref{pb:min_gen .}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{algo:FB} {l} x_0 \in \mathbb R^N for  k = 0, 1, \ldots \left\lfloor {l} x_{k+1} = \prox_{\tau p} \big( x_k - \tau A^*(A x_k - y) \big),  \right. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-024: In~\eqref{algo:FB , one has to compute the proximity operator of $p$ (defined in~\eqref{def:prox ) at each iteration.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: This operator can have an explicit formula for many simple choices of functions $p$ (see e.g.~\cite{combettes2011proximal ).}
elements.sent-2411-026: However this is not true in many cases of interest, and the proximity operator must be approximated numerically with sub-iterations.
elements.sent-2411-027: These sub-iterations can become computationally expensive in practice as they need to accurately approximate $\prox_p$.
elements.sent-2411-028: Recently, to improve the reconstruction quality and avoid these sub-iterations, proximal algorithms have also been paired with deep learning techniques for solving inverse imaging problems.
elements.sent-2411-029: Examples include deep dictionary learning (DDL)~\cite{SEM19 , Plug-and-Play (PnP) algorithms~\cite{brifman2016turning , and unfolded neural networks~\cite{GL10 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: However, providing convergence guarantees for these methods is not straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-031: A common prior's choice is based on the sparsity of the unknown signal in a certain basis, with prior $p$ of the form of \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_p} (\forall x \in \Rset^N)\quad p(x) = \lambda g(\Dica x),\end{equation} where $\lambda>0$ is a regularization parameter balancing data fidelity and regularization terms, $\Dica \colon \Rset^N \to \Rset^S$ is a linear operator modeling a sparsifying transformed domain, and $g\colon \Rset^S \to \Rset^N$ is a convex, lower semi-continuous, proper function promoting sparsity in the transformed domain (e.g., $\ell^1$ norm).
elements.sent-2411-032: Notable choices for the sparsifying operator include the Fourier, DCT, or Wavelet transforms~\cite{Mallat2008 as well as the Total Variation (TV) regularization~\cite{rudin1992nonlinear, chambolle2010introduction , depending on the properties of the target solution.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The structure of the sought signal, through the operator $\Dica$, can also be learned from a ground truth dataset in a supervised setting, leading to dictionary learning (DL) approaches~\cite{AEB06, MBPS09 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: For such sparsity-based priors, there exists no close-form formula and one typically needs to resort to expansive sub-iterations.
elements.sent-2411-035: In this paper, we investigate the properties of such approaches, strategies to reduce the sub-iteration computational burden, and their convergence.
elements.sent-2411-036: We adopt a PnP framework, where the proximity operator of $p$ in~\eqref{algo:FB is replaced with a denoiser.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Building our PnP algorithm with FB iterations, we investigate the behavior of the following FB-PnP scheme \begin{equation}\label{algo:FB-PnP} \begin{array}{l} x_0 \in \mathbb R^N \text{for } k = 0, 1, \ldots \left\lfloor \begin{array}{l} x_{k+1} = G \big( x_k - \tau A^*(A x_k - y) \big), \end{array} \right. \end{array}\end{equation} where $\tau>0$ and $G\colon \mathbb R^N \to \mathbb R^N$ is a sparsity-based denoising operator.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{algo:FB-PnP} {l} x_0 \in \mathbb R^N for  k = 0, 1, \ldots \left\lfloor {l} x_{k+1} = G \big( x_k - \tau A^*(A x_k - y) \big),  \right. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-038: Specifically, we consider $G$ to be either an analysis or a synthesis dictionary-based denoiser.
elements.sent-2411-040: On the one hand, the Analysis Denoiser (AD) is built as \begin{equation}\label{pb:AF-intro} \text{find } \quad G_{\Dica} (v) \approx \prox_{p}(v) = \argmind{x \in \Rset^N} \frac12 \| x - v \|^2 + g_\lambda(\Dica x),\end{equation} where $g_\lambda \equiv \lambda g$ and $\Dica \colon \mathbb R^N \to \mathbb R^S$.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{pb:AF-intro} find  \quad G_{\Dica} (v) \approx \prox_{p}(v) = \argmind{x \in \Rset^N} \frac12 \| x - v \|^2 + g_\lambda(\Dica x),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-041: On the other hand, the Synthesis Denoiser (SD) is built as \begin{equation}\label{pb:SF-intro} \text{find} \quad G_{\Dics}(v) \approx \Dics \, \argmind{z \in \Rset^S} \frac12 \| \Dics z-v \|^2 + \lambda g(z),\end{equation} where $\Dics \colon \mathbb R^S \to \mathbb R^N$.
elements.sent-2411-042: In general these two classes of denoisers do not have a closed-form formula (unless for specific cases, e.g., when $\Dica$ is (semi)-orthogonal~\cite{EMR07 ), thus requiring sub-iterations to compute their output.}
elements.sent-2411-043: We then aim to investigate the behavior of ${(x_k) _{k\in \mathbb N}$ when $G_{\Dica}$ and $G_{\Dics}$ are computed with FB-based iterations following an unrolling framework.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Our first contribution is to analyze the minimization problems associated with the proposed AD and SD described in~\eqref{pb:AF-intro -\eqref{pb:SF-intro , when $\Dica$ and $\Dics$ are fixed dictionaries.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: In particular, we show that~\eqref{pb:SF-intro corresponds to a proximity operator, and that the associated problem is equivalent to a synthesis formulation of problem~\eqref{pb:min_gen -\eqref{eq:def_p .}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Second, we investigate the behavior of algorithm~\eqref{algo:FB-PnP , where $G_{\Dica}$ and $G_{\Dics}$ are obtained with either $1$ sub-iteration of a FB-based scheme, or when a large enough number of these iterations are run for solving~\eqref{pb:AF-intro -\eqref{pb:SF-intro , leveraging a warm-restart strategy.}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: We further investigate the behavior of a similar algorithm to~\eqref{algo:FB-PnP for solving a Moreau envelope smoothing of problem~\eqref{pb:min_gen -\eqref{eq:def_p .}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: In this context, we show that using a warm-restart strategy yield an algorithm which converges toward a solution of the smooth approximated problem, if the operators $G_{\Dica}$ and $G_{\Dics}$ satisfy some sufficient decrease conditions.
elements.sent-2411-049: Finally, we show through simulations that the proposed unrolling within PnP frameworks is well suited to adopt a DDL approach for learning dictionaries $\Dica$ or~$\Dics$.

elements.subsection-2411-009: Stability analysis of the FB-PnP iterations
elements.sent-2411-258: In this section we aim to analyze the stability of the FB-PnP iterations when using the proposed AD or SD, depending on the number of layers $L$.
elements.sent-2411-259: Specifically, we want to illustrate the results presented in Theorem~\ref{thm:analysis_warm_restart and in Theorem~\ref{thm:cvg-SF-L1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-260: Although these results only hold when $L=1$ of $L\to \infty$ (i.e., the proximity operators are computed accurately), we will also empirically investigate the behavior of the AD and SD when $1 < L < +\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-261: To this aim, we consider a toy example consisting of an equation system of the form of $y = A \overline{x}$, where $\overline{x \in \Rset^N$ is a vector of dimension $N=50$ generated randomly following a uniform distribution, $A \colon \Rset^N \to \Rset^M$ is a Gaussian measurement operator with $M=20$, where coefficients are generated randomly following a Gaussian distribution with mean $0$ and standard deviation $1$.}
elements.sent-2411-262: Both the analysis and synthesis dictionaries are generated as Gaussian dictionaries with mean $0$ and standard deviation $1$, of size $\Dica \in \Rset^{100 \times 50}$ and $\Dics \in \Rset^{50 \times 100}$, respectively.

elements.subsection-2411-010: Application to image deblurring within a DDL framework
elements.sent-2411-280: Although the results presented in this work are generic and can be applied for any linear operators $\Dica$ and $\Dics$, they are of particular interest in the DDL framework, where these operators are learned.
elements.sent-2411-281: We will explore this case in this section, and apply the resulting methods to an image deblurring problem.
elements.sent-2411-282: For the sake of completeness we will compare the resulting algorithms to a FB-PnP algorithm where the denoiser is a DRUnet.
elements.sent-2411-283: As mentioned above, in this section, we focus on a deep learning approach, where the dictionaries $\Dica$ and $\Dics$ (see \Cref{Sec:AD-PnP and \Cref{Sec:SD-PnP for details) are learned as Gaussian denoisers, within a supervised context.}}
elements.sent-2411-284: Such an approach using unrolled algorithms for dictionary learning in computational imaging has been investigated in multiple works of the literature, either for the analysis formulation~\cite{chambolle2020learning, LPM20 or the synthesis formulation~\cite{TDB20, SEM19 , leading to the so-called DDL framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-285: In this section, we describe the training process for $\Dica$ and $\Dics$.
elements.sent-2411-286: The objective is to find an estimate $x^\dagger \in \Rset^N$ of an original unknown signal $\overline{x \in \Rset^N$ that has been corrupted by some additive white Gaussian noise.}
elements.sent-2411-287: Hence the noisy observed signal is given by \begin{equation}\label{pb:inv-den} v = \overline{x} + \upsilon w,\end{equation} where $w \in \mathbb R^N$ is a realization of an i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-288: Both analysis and synthesis formulations to find the estimate of $\overline{x}$ from $v$ in \eqref{pb:inv-den (see \eqref{pb:AF-inner and \eqref{pb:SF-inner , respectively) have been extensively studied in the literature (see e.g.~\cite{peyre2011learning, YNGD13 for the analysis, and~\cite{Olshausen1997, AEB06, MBPS09 for the synthesis formulation).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-289: In the context of DDL, these problems can be reformulated as a joint estimation of the image and the dictionary, or more generally as bi-level optimization problems~\cite{MBPS09 .}
elements.sent-2411-290: Then, the dictionaries are defined as solution to \begin{equation}\Dica^\dagger = \argmind{\Dica \in \Rset^{S \times N}} \ell^{\Am} \big( x^\dagger_{\Dica} \big) \quad  where  \quad x^\dagger_{\Dica} \text{ solution to~\eqref{pb:AF-inner}} \label{eqn:opt_analysis}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\Dics^\dagger = \argmind{\Dics \in \Rset^{N \times S}} \ell^{\Sm} \big( z^\dagger_{\Dics} \big) \quad  where  \quad z^\dagger_{\Dics} \text{ solution to~\eqref{pb:SF-inner}} \label{eqn:opt_synthesis}\end{equation} respectively, where $\ell^{\Am}$ and $\ell^{\Sm}$ are some loss functions.
elements.formula-2411-079: \begin{equation}\Dica^\dagger = \argmind{\Dica \in \Rset^{S \times N}} \ell^{\Am} \big( x^\dagger_{\Dica} \big) \quad  where  \quad x^\dagger_{\Dica} \text{ solution to~\eqref{pb:AF-inner}} \label{eqn:opt_analysis}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-080: \begin{equation}\Dics^\dagger = \argmind{\Dics \in \Rset^{N \times S}} \ell^{\Sm} \big( z^\dagger_{\Dics} \big) \quad  where  \quad z^\dagger_{\Dics} \text{ solution to~\eqref{pb:SF-inner}} \label{eqn:opt_synthesis}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-291: In this context, the computation of the signal $x^\dagger_{\Dica}$ or the sparse codes $z^\dagger_{\Dics}$ is often referred to the inner problem, while the global minimization for estimating the dictionary $\Dica$ or $\Dics$ is called the outer problem.
elements.sent-2411-292: If in~\eqref{eqn:opt_analysis (resp.~\eqref{eqn:opt_synthesis ), the loss function $\ell^{\Am (\Dica)$ is the same as the one minimized in~\eqref{pb:AF-inner (resp.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-293: This choice is often used in an unsupervised setting.
elements.sent-2411-294: In a supervised setting, where a ground truth dataset can be used, a standard choice for $\ell^{\Am}$ and $\ell^{\Sm}$ is the mean square error (MSE) loss, minimizing the $\ell^2$ norm of the difference between the ground truth and the estimated signal $x^\dagger_{\Dica}$ or $x^\dagger_{\Dics}$.
elements.sent-2411-295: In the remainder, we will use this approach.
elements.sent-2411-296: Specifically, we aim to learn $\Dica$ and $\Dics$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:def-unroll-learn} \begin{cases} x^\dagger_{\Am} = \NN^{\Am}_{L,\upsilon, v}(\Dica^*v) \approx \overline{x}, x^\dagger_{\Sm} = \NN^{\Sm}_{L,\upsilon, v}(\Dics^*v) \approx \overline{x} \end{cases}\end{equation} where $\NN^{\Am}$ and $\NN^{\Sm}$ are defined in Model~\ref{mod:AF and Model~\ref{mod:SF , respectively, for $L\ge 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-297: Furthermore, we assume that the dictionaries $\Dica$ and $\Dics$ are parametrized by learnable parameters $\theta_{\Dica}$ and $\theta_{\Dics}$, respectively (i.e., convolution kernel).
elements.sent-2411-298: Then, given a dataset of pairs of clean/noisy images ${(\overline{x _i, v_i) _{ i \in \mathbb{S _{\mathcal{T }$ obtained following~\eqref{pb:inv-den , the unrolled denoisers in \eqref{eq:def-unroll-learn are trained by solving, respectively \begin{equation}\minimize{\theta_{\Dica}} \frac{1}{\#(\mathbb{S}_{\mathcal{T}})} \sum_{i \in \mathbb{S}_{\mathcal{T}}} \| \NN^{\Am}_{\Ltrain, \upsilon_i, v_i}(\Dica^* v_i) - \overline{x}_i \|,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\minimize{\theta_{\Dics}} \frac{1}{\#(\mathbb{S}_{\mathcal{T}})} \sum_{i \in \mathbb{S}_{\mathcal{T}}} \| \NN^{\Sm}_{\Ltrain, \upsilon_i, v_i}(0_S) - \overline{x}_i \|,\end{equation} where $0_S \in \Rset^S$ is a constant vector with $0$ value on all coefficients.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-299: We trained our models on patches of size $150 \times 150$ of images from the training dataset from the BSDS500 image bank and we chose a noise level for the training of $\upsilon=0.05$.
elements.sent-2411-300: The learnable parameters $\theta_{\Dica}$ and $\theta_{\Dics}$ correspond to $50$ convolutional filters of dimension $5 \times 5$, and we fix $\sigma = \frac{1.8 {||\Gamma||^2}$ in Model~\ref{mod:AF and $\zeta=\frac{1.8 {||D||^2}$ in Model~\ref{mod:SF .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-301: We tested two strategies: either training the denoisers with only $\Ltrain=1$ iteration or with $\Ltrain=20$ iterations that share the same dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-302: In \Cref{tab:inference-time we give the inference time for denoising an image, when applying the AD and SD with either $\Lden=1$ or $\Lden=20$ iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-303: For comparison, we also provide the inference time when using a DRUnet, where the weights have been taken from the \href{https://deepinv.github.io/deepinv/ {DeepInv PyTorch library.}}
elements.sent-2411-304: We further provide examples of output images for the different denoisers in \Cref{fig:denoise-example , evaluated in different configurations.}
elements.sent-2411-305: It can be observed that the cases where SD is evaluated with $\Lden=1$ iteration (SD train $\Ltrain=1,20$ and $\Lden=1$) lead to very poor results.
elements.sent-2411-306: When SD is trained with $\Ltrain=1$ and evaluated with $\Lden=20$ iterations, results start to improve, although remaining quite poor.

elements.sent-2411-350: In this work we study convergence behavior of the FB-PnP algorithm where the proximity operator is replaced by a specific unfolded denoiser.
elements.sent-2411-351: In particular, we study two strategies: when the denoising problem is solved with an analysis formulation or when it is solved with a synthesis formulation.
elements.sent-2411-352: The two resulting analysis and the synthesis denoising minimization problems can then be solved with sub-iterations based on the dual FB algorithm and the FB algorithm, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-353: For both strategies, we show that computing only one sub-iteration within the FB-PnP combined with a warm-restart procedure on the sparse coefficients is equivalent to the FB-PnP algorithm when the full analysis or the synthesis denoising minimization problems are solved at each iteration.
elements.sent-2411-354: We further show that when smoothing the denoising problem using a Moreau envelope, this equivalence extends to an arbitrary number of sub-iterations.
elements.sent-2411-355: Finally, we investigated how these different strategies compare on a toy compressive sensing problem as well as on a deblurring imaging problem.. \bibliographystyle{plain.}

elements.sent-2411-356: Specifically, we consider both analysis and synthesis Gaussian denoisers within a dictionary framework, obtained by unrolling dual-FB iterations or FB iterations, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-357: This algorithm reads \begin{equation}\label{algo:FB} \begin{array}{l} x_0 \in \mathbb R^N \text{for } k = 0, 1, \ldots \left\lfloor \begin{array}{l} x_{k+1} = \prox_{\tau p} \big( x_k - \tau A^*(A x_k - y) \big), \end{array} \right. \end{array}\end{equation} where $\prox_{\tau p}$ is the so-called proximity operator of $p$ (see \eqref{def:prox for its definition), and $A^*$ denotes the adjoint operator of $A$.}
elements.sent-2411-358: In \eqref{algo:FB , $\tau>0$ is a step-size chosen to ensure the convergence of sequence ${(x_k) _{k\in \Nset}$ to a solution to~\eqref{pb:min_gen .}}}
elements.sent-2411-359: Examples include deep dictionary learning (DDL), Plug-and-Play (PnP) algorithms, and unfolded neural networks (see, e.g., \cite{SEM19, brifman2016turning, kamilov2023plug, martin2024pnp, GL10, JP2020 and references therein).}
elements.sent-2411-360: A common prior's choice is based on the sparsity of the unknown signal in a certain basis.
elements.sent-2411-361: In this context, the prior $p$ takes the form of \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_p} (\forall x \in \Rset^N)\quad p(x) = \lambda g(\Dica x),\end{equation} where $\lambda>0$ is a regularization parameter balancing data fidelity and regularization terms, $\Dica \colon \Rset^N \to \Rset^S$ is a linear operator modeling a sparsifying transformed domain, and $g\colon \Rset^S \to \Rset$ is a convex, lower semi-continuous, proper function.
elements.sent-2411-362: The function $g$ is then chosen for promoting sparsity in the transformed domain (e.g., $\ell^1$ norm).
elements.sent-2411-363: Notable choices for the sparsifying operator $\Dica$ include the Fourier, DCT, or Wavelet transforms~\cite{Mallat2008 as well as the Total Variation (TV) regularization~\cite{rudin1992nonlinear, chambolle2010introduction , depending on the properties of the target solution.}}
elements.sent-2411-364: For such sparsity-based priors, there exists no close-form formula and one typically needs to resort to expansive sub-iterations as well as inexact proximal methods allowing for computational errors to approximate the proximity operator (see, e.g., \cite{Combettes2005 ).}
elements.sent-2411-365: In this paper, we investigate the properties of such approaches, reducing the sub-iteration computational burden.
elements.sent-2411-366: We will in particular study convergence properties of the FB iterations~\eqref{algo:FB when the proximity operator is (very) roughly approximated.}
elements.sent-2411-367: We adopt a PnP framework, where the proximity operator of $p$ in~\eqref{algo:FB is replaced by a denoiser.}
elements.sent-2411-368: On the one hand, the Analysis Denoiser (AD) is built as \begin{equation}\label{pb:AF-intro} \text{find } \quad G_{\Dica} (v) \approx \prox_{g_\lambda \circ \Dica}(v) = \argmind{x \in \Rset^N} \frac12 \| x - v \|^2 + g_\lambda(\Dica x),\end{equation} where $g_\lambda \equiv \lambda g$ and $\Dica \colon \mathbb R^N \to \mathbb R^S$.
elements.formula-2411-084: \begin{equation}\label{pb:AF-intro} find  \quad G_{\Dica} (v) \approx \prox_{g_\lambda \circ \Dica}(v) = \argmind{x \in \Rset^N} \frac12 \| x - v \|^2 + g_\lambda(\Dica x),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-369: On the other hand, the Synthesis Denoiser (SD) is built as \begin{equation}\label{pb:SF-intro} \text{find} \quad G_{\Dics}(v) = \Dics z^\dagger_{\Dics} \;\text{ where }\; z^\dagger_{\Dics} \approx \argmind{z \in \Rset^S} \frac12 \| \Dics z-v \|^2 + g_\lambda(z),\end{equation} where $\Dics \colon \mathbb R^S \to \mathbb R^N$.
elements.formula-2411-085: \begin{equation}\label{pb:SF-intro} find \quad G_{\Dics}(v) = \Dics z^\dagger_{\Dics} \; where \; z^\dagger_{\Dics} \approx \argmind{z \in \Rset^S} \frac12 \| \Dics z-v \|^2 + g_\lambda(z),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-370: Our first contribution is to analyze the minimization problems associated with the proposed AD and SD in~\eqref{pb:AF-intro -\eqref{pb:SF-intro when $\Dica$ and $\Dics$ are fixed dictionaries.}}
elements.sent-2411-371: In particular we show that~\eqref{pb:SF-intro corresponds to a proximity operator, and that the associated problem is equivalent to a synthesis formulation of problem~\eqref{pb:min_gen -\eqref{eq:def_p (see Section~\ref{Ssec:SF-equiv ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-372: Second, we investigate the behavior of algorithm~\eqref{algo:FB-PnP , where $G_{\Dica}$ and $G_{\Dics}$ are obtained running $1$ sub-iteration of a FB-based scheme for solving~\eqref{pb:AF-intro -\eqref{pb:SF-intro , using a warm-restart strategy (see Sections~\ref{Sec:AD-PnP and~\ref{Sec:SD-PnP , respectively).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-373: Specifically, we show that the iterations using the AD within a FB-PnP with only $1$ sub-iteration of a dual FB algorithm \cite{combettes2010dualization are equivalent to those of the Loris-Verhoeven primal-dual algorithm~\cite{loris2011generalization .}}
elements.sent-2411-374: Similarly, we show that the iterations using the SD within a FB-PnP with only $1$ sub-iteration of a FB algorithm are equivalent to the FB iterations for solving the full synthesis formulation of problem~\eqref{pb:min_gen -\eqref{eq:def_p .}}
elements.sent-2411-375: In this context, we show that using a warm-restart strategy yields an algorithm which converges toward a solution of the smooth approximated problem, if the operators $G_{\Dica}$ and $G_{\Dics}$ satisfy some sufficient decrease conditions (see Section~\ref{sec:bi-level-approximate ).}
elements.sent-2411-376: Finally, we illustrate our theoretical study on two simulation examples: a toy compressive sensing example, and an example on image restoration.
elements.sent-2411-377: In particular, we show through simulations that the proposed framework is well suited to adopt a DDL approach for learning dictionaries $\Dica$ or~$\Dics$ (see Section~\ref{Sec:exp ).}
elements.sent-2411-378: A summary of the methods investigated in this work is provided in Table~\ref{tab:algo_sum , and their associated theoretical conditions for convergence are summarized in Table~\ref{tab:algo_sum_cvg .}}

elements.sent-2411-550: With our notation, the scaled Loris-Verhoeven primal-dual algorithm proposed in~\cite[Section~6, Eq.~(40)]{loris2011generalization is designed to $\text{find \widehat{x \in \Argmind{x \in \Rset^N \frac12 \| Ax - y \|^2 + g_\lambda(\Dica x)$, which is the same problem as stated in~\eqref{pb:inv-min-AF .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-551: The iterations to solve this problem are given by \begin{equation}\begin{array}{l} \text{for } k = 0, 1, \ldots \left\lfloor \begin{array}{l} \widetilde{x}_{k+1} = x_k - \tau A^*(Ax_k - y) - \tau \Dica^* \widetilde{u}_k \widetilde{u}_{k+1} = \prox_{\frac{\sigma}{\tau} g_\lambda^*}( \widetilde{u}_k + \frac{\sigma}{\tau} \Dica \widetilde{x}_{k+1} ) x_{k+1} = x_k - \tau A^*(Ax_k - y) - \tau \Dica^* \widetilde{u}_{k+1}, \end{array} \right. \end{array}\end{equation} with $\tau<2/\|A^*A\|$ and $\sigma<1/\|\Dica^*\Dica\|$.

elements.sent-2411-552: DRUnet is a noise-aware denoising network that has been proposed in~\cite{zhang2021plug .}
elements.sent-2411-553: It has a U-Net like structure, with convolutional blocks in the encoder and decoder parts, and it takes into account the noise level of the input image as an extra input channel.
elements.sent-2411-554: It is considered as a state-of-the-art denoising network to use in PnP frameworks, and trained DRUnet can be found on PyTorch-based libraries such as \href{https://deepinv.github.io/deepinv/user_guide/reconstruction/denoisers.html {DeepInv .}}
elements.sent-2411-555: Then, if $G \colon \Rset^N \times \Rset^N \to \Rset^N$ denotes the DRunet operator, the corresponding FB-PnP iterations are given as \begin{equation}\label{algo:FB-PnP-drunet} \begin{array}{l} \text{for } k = 0, 1, \ldots \left\lfloor \begin{array}{l} x_{k+1} = G \big( x_k - \tau A^*(A x_k - y), \, \lambda \tau \operatorname{Id}_N \big), \end{array} \right. \end{array}\end{equation} where $\lambda>0$ denotes the input noise level that acts as a regularization parameter on the network, and $\tau>0$ is the step-size chosen to ensure convergence of $(x_k)_{k\in \mathbb N}$.
